questions.question: Prove that in any binary tree with n nodes, exactly n+1 pointer fields contain NULL values.
keyPoints: A binary tree with n nodes has exactly 2n pointer fields (left and right for each node)
Each node except the root has exactly one incoming edge from its parent, giving n-1 non-NULL pointers
Total NULL pointers = Total pointers - Non-NULL pointers = 2n - (n-1) = n+1
This holds for trees of all shapes: complete, full, or skewed

questions.question: Explain the difference between single-threaded and double-threaded binary trees. What are the advantages and disadvantages of each?
keyPoints: Single-threaded uses only one direction (left or right) for threading, saving less memory
Double-threaded uses both directions, enabling bi-directional traversal
Advantage of single: Less overhead (only 1 flag), simpler structure
Advantage of double: Complete traversal capability in both directions
Disadvantage of single: Can only traverse in one direction efficiently
Disadvantage of double: More flags per node (2 vs 1)

questions.question: Describe the algorithm for inorder traversal in a threaded binary tree without using recursion or a stack.
keyPoints: Start by finding the leftmost node (keep going left until NULL)
Visit the current node
If rthread = 1, follow right pointer to successor and repeat step 2
If rthread = 0, move to right child and go to leftmost descendant
Continue until NULL is reached

questions.question: Given a binary tree with 15 nodes, calculate: (a) Number of NULL pointers in conventional representation, (b) Number of pointers saved after converting to doubly-threaded tree, (c) Additional space required for flags.
keyPoints: (a) For n=15, NULL pointers = n+1 = 16
(b) All 16 NULL pointers become threads, so 16 pointers saved
(c) Each node needs 2 flag bits, so 15 × 2 = 30 bits = approximately 4 bytes (if stored as bytes) or 15 × 2 booleans

questions.question: Explain the node structure of a doubly-threaded binary tree. How do the flags help distinguish between child pointers and threads?
keyPoints: Node contains: left pointer, lthread flag, data, rthread flag, right pointer
lthread = 0: left pointer points to left child; lthread = 1: left pointer points to inorder predecessor
rthread = 0: right pointer points to right child; rthread = 1: right pointer points to inorder successor
Flags solve the ambiguity of whether a pointer is a child or a thread

questions.question: Insert the following keys in order into an initially empty threaded binary search tree: 20, 10, 30, 5, 15, 25, 35. Show the final tree structure and identify all thread connections.
keyPoints: Insert 20 as root
Insert 10: goes left of 20 (no left child), becomes left child with threads
Insert 30: goes right of 20 (no right child), becomes right child
Insert 5: goes left of 10, then left of 5's parent becomes leftmost with thread to predecessor
Insert 15: goes right of 10, left of 20 - position found
Insert 25: goes left of 30, right of 20
Insert 35: goes right of 30 - rightmost
After all insertions, update all threads: leftmost node (5) has left thread to NULL, right thread to 10; rightmost node (35) has right thread to NULL
All NULL pointers replaced with inorder predecessor/successor links

questions.question: Write an algorithm to insert a new node as the left child of a given parent in a doubly-threaded binary tree. Include thread updates and analyze the time complexity.
keyPoints: Algorithm steps: Create new node with lthread=1, rthread=1 initially
Set newnode->left = parent->left (old subtree)
Set newnode->right = parent (new node's successor is parent)
If parent had no left child (parent->lthread=1): update predecessor's right thread to point to newnode
Set parent->left = newnode, parent->lthread = 0
Time complexity: O(h) where h is height, worst case O(n) for skewed tree
Finding position dominates; actual insertion and thread updates are O(1)

questions.question: Compare conventional binary trees and threaded binary trees in terms of: (a) Memory usage, (b) Traversal complexity, (c) Operations complexity (search, insert, delete). Provide a mathematical analysis.
keyPoints: (a) Memory: Conventional uses 2n pointers (all used or NULL). Threaded uses 2n pointers + 2n flag bits, but saves n+1 NULL pointers. Net: Threaded saves (n+1) pointers worth of space minus overhead of 2n bits
(b) Traversal: Conventional needs O(n) stack space + O(n) time. Threaded needs O(1) extra space + O(n) time. Both O(n) time but threaded is space-efficient

(c) Search: Both O(h), same complexity. Insert/Delete: Conventional O(h). Threaded also O(h) for finding position plus O(1) for thread updates. Threaded deletion is more complex due to thread maintenance
